coredata.dc:title: Rapid colorimetric flow injection sensing of hypochlorite by functionalized graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Baghi Sefidan, Somayyeh
Eskandari, Habibollah
Shamkhali, Amir Nasser
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with oxygen-containing functional groups along their edges have been prepared by controlled thermalization of citric acid to develop a new spectrophotometric reagent for hypochlorite determination. 2, 4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) was reacted with hypochlorite to form 2, 4-dinitrophenyl diazonium cation (DNPDC) in acidic condition through a flow injection manifold and then coupled to GQDs to produce a colored product. Characterization of the appropriate materials were performed by applying different imaging and spectroscopic techniques. The results obtained from the CCD optimization approach revealed that all of the chemical reagents had a significant role individually while their interactions were not of any importance. Under optimum condition, Beer’s law was obeyed for hypochlorite determination at 423 nm in the range of 0.2–12.3 μg mL−1. The obtained detection limit of hypochlorite was 0.1 μg mL−1. Also, the intra-day experiments (n = 10 at 0.7 and 11.0 μg mL−1 of hypochlorite) showed recoveries of 100.2 and 99.8%, respectively and that of inter-day (n = 5 at 0.7 and 11.0 μg mL−1 of hypochlorite) presented, respectively, 99.3 and 99.5% recoveries. The sample throughput was about 100 h−1. Tap and pool water samples as well as bleach solutions were analyzed by this and other appropriate reference analytical methods. No statistical differences were observed between the results of the presented method and the results obtained by the reference methods. Beside the studies, a model structure of a typical functionalized GQD was optimized using density functional theory. The natural charges on the carbons along the edge of GQD were calculated, and the results indicated that the ortho positions with respect to the phenolic OH moieties are the most preferable sites for the electrophilic substitution reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Central composite design
Density functional theory
Hypochlorite
2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine
Flow injection analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the synthesized GQDs and GODs
Optimization of chemical and instrumental parameters
Assessment of different chemicals as coupling agents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The hypochlorite-DNPH-GQDs reaction to produce the yellow colored product.
TEM (I), DLS (II) and Raman spectrum (III) of GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of: citric acid (a), the synthesized GQDs (b), the synthesized GODs (c), DNPH (d) and the formed product (e).

Investigation on PH and DNPH as the phenylhydrazine precursors. effect of the diazotization period (I) and coupling period of PH (II), coupling period of DNPH (III) and sensitivity comparison in FIA set up for PH and DNPH (IV).
Condition for (I-III): 10.0 mL aqueous solution (13.7 μg mL−1 hypochlorite) containing PH or DNPH 0.3 mmol L-1, HCl 0.17 mol L-1 and 0.84 mg mL-1 GQDS.
Condition for (IV): as ascribed in Scheme 1 (hypochlorite as 10.0 μg mL−1).
Effect of DNPH (I), hydrochloric acid (II) and GQDs (III) on the sensitivity of the method.

Condition: for DNPH optimization, different DNPH concentrations in 0.3 mol L−1 HCl, GQDs 2.52 mg mL−1, L1 equal to 80 cm, L2 equal to 40 cm, 250 μL of hypochlorite as13.7 μg mL−1 and flow rate of 1.3 mL min−1 were applied. For optimization of HCl and GQDs their concentrations were varied, respectively (as mentioned in the text).
Three-dimensional (3D) response surfaces obtained based on the model for optimization of chemical variables.
Condition: L1 equal to 10 cm, L2 equal to 40 cm, 250 μL of hypochlorite solution as 13.7 μg mL−1 and flow rate as 1.6 mL min−1 were applied.
Electron density on the carbons of the GQD model.
Levels of factors for central composite design.
Analysis of variance for the experiments in Table S1.
Different reagents tested as the coupling agent.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Condition: R2, GODs (2.98-fold diluted) in optimized chemical and instrumental condition.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Condition: R2, GQDs (2.98-fold diluted) in optimized chemical and instrumental condition.

0.7 μg mL−1
11.0 μg mL−1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of free chlorine in bleaches.
Determination of hypochlorite in pool water samples.
Nanoparticle based methods for detection of hypochlorite.

head.ce:title: Rapid colorimetric flow injection sensing of hypochlorite by functionalized graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil 56199-11367, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with oxygen-containing functional groups along their edges have been prepared by controlled thermalization of citric acid to develop a new spectrophotometric reagent for hypochlorite determination. 2, 4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) was reacted with hypochlorite to form 2, 4-dinitrophenyl diazonium cation (DNPDC) in acidic condition through a flow injection manifold and then coupled to GQDs to produce a colored product. Characterization of the appropriate materials were performed by applying different imaging and spectroscopic techniques. The results obtained from the CCD optimization approach revealed that all of the chemical reagents had a significant role individually while their interactions were not of any importance. Under optimum condition, Beer’s law was obeyed for hypochlorite determination at 423 nm in the range of 0.2–12.3 μg mL−1. The obtained detection limit of hypochlorite was 0.1 μg mL−1. Also, the intra-day experiments (n = 10 at 0.7 and 11.0 μg mL−1 of hypochlorite) showed recoveries of 100.2 and 99.8%, respectively and that of inter-day (n = 5 at 0.7 and 11.0 μg mL−1 of hypochlorite) presented, respectively, 99.3 and 99.5% recoveries. The sample throughput was about 100 h−1. Tap and pool water samples as well as bleach solutions were analyzed by this and other appropriate reference analytical methods. No statistical differences were observed between the results of the presented method and the results obtained by the reference methods. Beside the studies, a model structure of a typical functionalized GQD was optimized using density functional theory. The natural charges on the carbons along the edge of GQD were calculated, and the results indicated that the ortho positions with respect to the phenolic OH moieties are the most preferable sites for the electrophilic substitution reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene like materials such as reduced graphene oxide and graphitic carbon nitrides are two dimensional materials with very important applications in chemistry [1–3] while graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are considered zero dimensional. They have found numerous applications in many scientific domains due to quantum confinement, aromatic structure and edge effects. GQDs and CDs are developed using different carbon precursors by a variety of chemical and physical manipulations when top-down and bottom-up procedures are applied [4,5]. Some experimental and theoretical studies reveal the effects of different functional groups on the chemical and physical characteristics and finally on the behavior of GQDs. Some of the moieties are -OH [6], -COOH [7], -COO− [6], -NH2 [8], -NO2 − [9], -F [10], -Br [7], −COCH3 [7], −OCH3 [7], −OCF = CF2 [7], −CH = CF2 [7], −CH = CH2 [7], -Cl [11], −COOCH3 [12], −CONH2 [13], −CO-N(CH3)2 [14,15], aryl groups [16], sulfur [17], folic acid [18] and valine [19]. Carbonization degree of citric acid may be controlled to prepare the GQD-containing hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups. This approach is one of the most efficient and economic synthetic methods to prepare chemically reactive GQDs [20].

Literature survey reveals that the most analytical spectroscopic applications of GQDs and carbon dots (CDs) are based on fluorescence quenching [5,17] where fluorescence of GQDs is measured, as the analytical response, for determination of analytes such as Ag(I) [17], Hg(II) [19], cyanide [21], free chlorine [22], dopamine [23], Fe(III) [24] and thiamine [25] in many samples. Up to now, no GQDs-based spectrophotometric analytical method has been developed.

Hypochlorite as a reactive oxygen compound cannot be disregarded in our life [26]. It is produced in body of organisms, has antimicrobial effect and regulates some physiological functions [27,28]. However, excess production of hypochlorite in body causes serious problems [26]. Bleach is frequently applied as a strong oxidizing and decolorizing agent and also as disinfectant in drinking and swimming pool water. Hypochlorite produces chlorate, chlorite and tri-halomethanes when it is applied as a disinfectant. The formed chemicals are harmful and carcinogen [26,29].

Hydrazine derivatives are used for the determination of hypochlorite. They are oxidized by hypochlorite in presence of hydrochloric acid to establish a titrimetric method for their determination [30]. One of the most important hydrazine derivatives are phenylhydrazines. They are oxidized to phenyldiazonium cations by hypochlorite in order to develop some more sensitive colorimetric hypochlorite determination methods. Hypochlorite decolorizes 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) to form 2, 4-dinitrophenyl diazonium cation (DNPDC) in order to assay hypochlorite [31]. Analytical chemists often prefer to measure an increasing instrumental response as the analytical response while concentration of the analyte is increasing. Some sensitive spectrophotometric chlorine determination methods have been developed based on azo-coupling reaction between the oxidized phenylhydrazine derivatives and a coupling agent. These methods have used phenylhydrazine-4-sulfonic acid and N-(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine (NED) [32,33], and 4-nitrophenylhydrazine and NED [34]. In the chlorine determination methods, liquid phase micro extraction [32], solid phase extraction [33] and flow injection [34] techniques were applied. Generally, different azo coupling agents show different analytical figures of merit.

Within the past two decades, density functional theory (DFT) method has been widely used for quantum chemical investigation on molecules in both gaseous, liquid states and even the solid-state materials due to its appropriate results with acceptable accuracy and lower computational cost in comparison with higher level ab initio methods. Several DFT studies on GQDs have been reported so far, some of which were focused on electronic structure of GQDs while others were concerned with edges of GQDs. For example, Voznyy et al. studied the effects of reconstruction and passivation of zigzag edges on electronic and magnetic properties of triangular GQDs [35]. They found that the effect of edge reconstruction on electronic and magnetic properties of GQDs depends on their size, and the band of zero-energy states is dispersed due to reduction of dot symmetry as a result of edge reconstruction [35]. Shi et al. used DFT method in order to obtain distribution of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of GQDs in the molecule and relate it to their physical properties [36] and indicated that the pattern formed by different distributions of the phase components of HOMO are unique for different geometric shapes, and are heavily affected by the orientations of the edges and corners of quantum dots. Moreover, they found that the wave function can be modified by substitution of appropriate functional groups around the circumference, which supports the changes in higher level electronic properties observed in these structures [36]. Also, Ghosh et al. studied the effect of edge hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the absorption spectrum of GQDs using DFT calculations [37]. Their calculations indicated that the excitation energies in visible region can be adjusted by introducing a certain number of OH or COOH groups [37]. Noori et al. investigated functionalization of GQD with ethylene-dinitrobenzoyl (EDNB) by DFT method [38] and concluded that both work function and optical spectrum of GQDs can be modified by linking EDNB to the phenyl edges via amine groups [38]. Wang et al. studied surface functionalization of GQDs with donor or acceptor groups by DFT method [39] which indicated that donor or acceptor groups are responsible for a red shift in the absorption spectrum, and electrons and holes are highly delocalized [39]. Geethalakshmi et al. used DFT calculations in order to study the effect of −OH functional groups on optical properties of GQDs [40]. Their results indicated that electronic properties of GQDs can be controlled by selective functionalization with OH groups. The hydroxyl groups decrease ionization potential of the GQDs in comparison with initial GQDs. Also, Charge transfer from and to the hydroxyl groups has been shown to have no important role in the fluorescence mechanism. They predicted that functionalization of the positions with larger electron–hole separation in the initial GQDs affects their properties, considerably. Their calculations indicated that Hydroxyl groups located on the basal plane have a considerable effect on the properties, while substitutions on the edge plane have a negligible effect on the properties of GQDs [40]. The mentioned DFT studies give us useful results for construction of GQDs with desired optical properties. However, there are not enough research as to reactive positions of GQDs for electrophilic and nucleophilic attacks from a theoretical point of view.

Flow injection (FI)-spectrophotometric based methods may be the best approaches to solve some of the problems owing to certain improvements in automation, sample manipulation, reproducibility, speed of analysis, economy and safety [41].

In the present investigation, an especially important analytical spectrophotometric application of GQDs is reported. In this flow injection-based method, DNPH is oxidized to its related aryl diazonium cation (DNPDC) by hypochlorite in hydrochloric acid stream. Thereafter, DNPDC is coupled with GQDs to produce a yellow colored product for spectrophotometric determination of hypochlorite. Through this research, a CCD approach has been used to elucidate the manner in which the chemical parameters affect the response function. Also, DFT calculations are carried out on the model structure of synthesized GQD whose results were used to identify the active positions of GQDs against electrophilic attacks in order to find more information about chemistry of the DNPDC-GQDs reaction.

Citric acid anhydrous, hydrochloric acid, potassium iodide, potassium iodate, acetic acid, starch, sodium thiosulfate, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (sodium salt of EDTA), mercuric chloride, anhydrous sodium hydrogen phosphate, anhydrous potassium dihydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, N, N'-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) sulfate, sulfuric acid, phenylhydrazine hydrochloride and DNPH were all obtained from (Merck, Germany). 1-Naphthol and N-(1-naphthyl)-ethylenediamine (NED) dihydrochloride were purchased from Loba-Chemie Company (India) and sodium hypochlorite solution (available chlorine as 5%) from SEHAT Company (Iran). Almost all the chemicals were of analytical grade reagents.

1000 mg L−1 fresh stock standard hypochlorite solution was prepared by diluting the commercially available hypochlorite solution in a 0.01 mol L−1 sodium hydroxide solution. The solution was standardized using an iodometric method with sodium thiosulfate [42]. Working standard hypochlorite solutions were made by rapid successive dilutions of the fresh stock solution.

A 0.02 mol L−1 DNPH ethanolic solution was prepared as stock in a 100.0 mL volumetric flask. The fresh working solutions of DNPH were prepared appropriately by diluting the stock solution with appropriate standard hydrochloric acid solutions.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) information was obtained by a Philips X'pert Pro X-ray diffractometer with a radiation source Cu Kα (λ = 0.1542 nm). To study the morphologies of the prepared GQDs and graphene oxide dots (GODs), a LEO 1430 V P scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a Zeiss EM10C electron transmission microscope were used. Particle size measurement was also performed by employing a Horiba SZ-100z dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument. Raman spectrum was obtained by using an Almega Thermo Nicolet dispersive Raman spectrometer.

A Perkin-Elmer Spectrum model RX-I was used for obtaining the FT-IR spectra. UV–vis spectra were recorded by utilizing a Shimadzu double beam spectrophotometer model UV-1650 PC equipped with a pair of matched quartz cells with 10.0 mm path length (Hellma). An oven (Beschickung-Loading Modell 100–800; Memmert) was used for thermal manipulation and material drying.

The applied FI manifold is given in Scheme S1. The FI system is composed of a three-channel peristaltic pump (Gilson, Minipuls 2) equipped with silicone rubber tubes (0.8 mm i.d.), PEEK mixing tees (VICI, USA) and 6-port-2-position low pressure injection valve (Valco, USA). The sample loop volume was 250 μL. Absorbance was measured with time by a Cecil spectrophotometer model CE 1021 equipped with a Hellma 18 μL flow-through cell (10.0 mm optical path). The working wavelength was set to 423 nm through the entire absorbance monitoring process.

The GQDs and GODs were synthesized by controlling the degree of citric acid carbonization. To prepare GQDs, 2.0 g citric acid was carbonized at 200 °C for 30 min. When the carbonization period was continued for 2 h, the color changed from orange to black which confirmed production of GODs. These synthesized matters (0.754 g GQDs and 0.361 g GODs) were dispersed separately into a sodium hydroxide solution, according to the method reported in [20]. Working solutions of the GQDs and GODs were prepared by appropriate dilution.

The hypochlorite (chlorine) contents of the samples were analyzed by using a three-line FI manifold which is described in Scheme S1. During the analysis, 250 μL of the samples were injected into the carrier stream.

Typically, it is required to evaluate the efficiency of the established method for analysis of the samples. The N, N'-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD) spectrophotometric procedure was utilized as a standard method for the analysis of pool water samples when 10.0 mL of the standards (to prepare calibration curve) or samples of hypochlorite solutions were mixed with phosphate buffer and DPD reagent solutions. Absorbance was read at 515 nm against blank [42,43]. Furthermore, an iodometric method was employed for the determination of hypochlorite in the bleach solutions which were diluted appropriately and titrated by a standard 0.1 mol L−1 sodium thiosulfate solution to reach a paled-yellow color. Then, starch was added and titration was continued to a blue-violet color [42,43].

All of DFT calculations were performed by B3LYP hybrid DFT functional using 6–31 G (d,p) basis set [44–46]. The model structure of GQD was optimized by the mentioned DFT method and basis set using Firefly quantum chemistry program [47,48]. Then, partial charges of atoms were obtained by natural bond orbital analysis (NBO) [49].
For data manipulation of the CCD experiments, a MINITAB statistical package (Minitab Inc. Release 17.0) was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized GQDs and GODs
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire information about the size and shape of the synthesized GQDs and GODs, SEM and TEM images were obtained. SEM images displayed relatively regular circular GQDs and GODs with average diameters about 20 nm and 100 nm, respectively (Fig. S1).

Fig. 1 (I) shows the TEM image of the prepared GQDs which indicated that the spherical GQDs have the average diameter about 15 nm mostly distributed in the range of 10–25 nm. These results are in good compatibility with the previously reported data [20]. The results obtained from TEM image are also consistent with the obtained DLS data (Fig. 1(II)) which showed a narrow distribution with an average size about 12.4 nm for GQDs.

Fig. 1(III) shows the Raman spectrum of GQDs which is a powerful and widely used technique to characterize the grapheme like structures. The Raman spectrum displays two prominent peaks of D-band and G-band at 1370 and 1605 cm−1, respectively. The D-band is related to the defects (sp3 carbons) whereas the G-band is attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbons. The intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) is used to define the degree of crystallinity of graphitic systems. Such a large ratio for ID/IG which was found to be 0.81 for GQDs indicates that GQDs have a similar structure to graphite and are crystalline. ID/IG values of GQDs vary significantly depending on the preparation methods [50,51].

FT-IR spectra were recorded for identification of functional groups in citric acid, synthesized GQDs and GODs. Also, IR spectrum for GQDs shows some functional groups evidently (Fig. 2 (b)). The broad stretching vibrations of OH (3100–3750 cm−1), CO stretching (1696 cm−1), COO vibration (1396 cm−1 (sym), 1562 cm−1 (asym)), and COH groups (1120 cm−1) were attributed to GQDs. When the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2(b and c)) are compared, some differences are observed. For instance: conversion of the COH group (stretching vibration at 1120 cm−1) in the GQDs to the COC group (stretching vibration at 1016 cm−1) in the GODs, triggering the reaction of GQDs (as intermediate) with each other leading to formation of larger dots (GODs) by longer thermalization period in GODs preparation than GQDs [20,52].

Also, the FT-IR spectra of the DNPH, GQDs and the resulting product are shown in Fig. 2(b), (d) and (e), respectively. The coupling in the resultant product is evident from the occurrence of a relatively strong absorption band in the FT-IR spectrum with the characteristic wave number of 1498 cm−1. This pattern corresponds to NN vibrations in azo dyes (Fig. 2(e)). Comparison of the FT-IR spectra for DNPH and the coupling product shows a common absorption band at 746 cm−1 that is attributed to the wagging of NO2 group (Fig. 2(d and e)).
As it is compared in Fig. S1(III), both synthesized GQDs and GODs exhibit the same XRD patterns in shape and strength which are ascribed to formation of graphite structures [20,53].
The colorimetric determination of hypochlorite was performed by oxidation of hydrazine moiety of DNPH with hypochlorite in hydrochloric acid medium to produce DNPDC prior to coupling with GQDs.

Some batch experiments were conducted where phenylhydrazine (PH) and DNPH were oxidized by hypochlorite in hydrochloric acid. Then, the formed diazonium cations were reacted with GQDs to form the related colored products. Oxidation period for PH (the time between addition of hypochlorite and GQDs) was varied in the range of 5–60 s. The resultant spectra are given in Fig. 3 (I) as overlay. The sensitivity was increased by increasing the oxidation period. Also, the period of coupling between oxidized pH and GQDs was varied. The results showed that the sensitivity increased substantially for 5-min period, and then remained constant (Fig. 3(II)). Other batch experiments were conducted by varying the reaction period between DNPH and hypochlorite. The obtained results indicated that DNPH reaction with hypochlorite was very fast (instantaneous). The period of coupling between oxidized form of DNPH and GQDs was also varied in the range of 2–50 min. The results are given in Fig. 3(III). At first, a maximum at 423 nm appeared, but increasing the period of coupling time caused the maximum to shift to lower wavelengths. In the FT-IR spectrum of the product (Fig. 2(e)), the characteristic absorption band attributed to -N = N- was observed (1498 cm−1). Oxidation of carbon-carbon double bonds of the GQDs moiety near the –N = N- [54] or possible aggregation of the product (that may change the optical characteristics of the formed product) [55,56] may contribute in the decolorizing.

The observations reveal that PH-GQDs system is applicable for batch hypochlorite determination. However, for flow injection assay which needs fast reactions, the limitation on sensitivity may be in place. On the other hand, stability of absorbance over time for DNPH-GQDs system may provide the limitation on reproducibility when a batch method is assembled for hypochlorite assay. Therefore, to develop a fast hypochlorite colorimetric determination method, DNPH-GQDs system based on a flow injection manifold is preferred to PH-GQDs system. In Fig. 3(IV) the sensitivities of both DNPH-GQDs and PH-GQDs systems are compared.

According to the principles of azo-coupling reactions, the aryl diazonium cation behaves as a weak electrophile. So, the aromatic coupling agents with amino or hydroxy functional groups are favored. In Scheme 1 , a typical structure for the employed GQDs is given as a model. The oxygen containing functional groups of the GQD have been distributed along the edges. Hydroxy and carboxy groups are considered as the electron donating and electron accepting groups [57], respectively. The possible sites of GQDs for the coupling reaction (whenever a diazonium cation attacks) have been assigned in Table 1 . Azo-coupling reaction takes place favorably in para position than ortho position of the aromatic coupling agents [58,59]. However, in GQDs the para position coupling sites are absent. According to the results in Table 1, the most reactive coupling sites (carbons with more negative electron densities) on the model GQD are the ortho positions with respect to the phenolic OH groups. The diazotization-coupling pattern is given in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of chemical and instrumental parameters

ce:section.ce:para: The assembled FI setup (illustrated in Scheme S1) was used for optimization. The concentrations of the employed chemicals, temperature and the instrumental parameters have been considered to be optimized. Absorbance as the peak height at 423 nm, resulting after injection of a known amount of hypochlorite, was measured and considered as the analytical response. A univariate optimization method was followed and all the measurements were performed at least three times.

Initially, chemical parameters were varied when the instrumental parameters and temperature were kept constant. Temperature, flow rate (for each channel) and sample injection volume were set as 20 °C, 1.3 mL min−1and 250 μL, respectively. The length of reaction coils, L1 and L2 were 80 cm (for oxidation of DNPH) and 40 cm (for coupling reaction with GQDs), respectively. The first chemical parameter, DNPH concentration, was varied and the obtained results are given in Fig. 4 (I). As it can be seen, the best sensitivity was observed for DNPH concentration of 3 × 10-4 mol L−1 (this DNPH concentration was selected as the optimized value). Then, concentrations of hydrochloric acid and GQDs were varied consecutively. The selected (optimized) values of hydrochloric acid and GQDs were as 0.3 mol L−1 and 2.52 mg mL−1 (3.0 fold diluted of the stock solution of GQDs), respectively (Fig. 4(II and III)).

The optimization process continued through focusing on the instrumental variables. To reach the maximum absorbance in the FI methods, the maximum reaction yield and the minimum dispersion must be attained. The reaction of DNPH with hypochlorite in the presence of hydrochloric acid takes place in L1 reaction coil. Also, the length of L2 determines the efficiency of coupling reaction between the oxidized form of DNPH and GQDs. Increasing the length of L2 increases the coupling period (it means more efficient coupling reaction) but causes more dispersion, and possible degradation and/or aggregation (of the formed product). Therefore, lengths of the reaction coils (L1 and L2) were optimized separately (Fig. S2(I and II) and the values of L1 and L2, respectively, were selected as 10 and 40 cm.

The effect of injected volume of hypochlorite into the FI manifold was investigated too. The injection volume was tested in the range of 20–500 μL. The sensitivity increased when sample volume was increased, but more sample volume led to wider peak and therefore more analytical time. Based on these considerations, sample volume as 250 μL was selected for the next studies (Fig. S2(III)).

The role of flow rate as an important instrumental factor was also investigated in the range of 0.33–2.75 mL min−1 for each channel. There was an increase in the peak height when the flow rate was varied up to 2.0 mL min−1. At higher flow rates, absorbance decreased due to reduction in the period of DNPH oxidation and coupling reactions (Fig. S2(IV)). Therefore, flow rate as 1.6 mL min−1 was selected as the optimum value.

Finally, the effects of temperature (in the range of 20–50 °C) and ionic strength (by adding sodium chloride in the range of 0.05–1.0 mol L−1) on the sensitivity of the method were investigated. The obtained results revealed that temperature and ionic strength had no significant effects on the sensitivity of the method. Therefore, temperature was kept at 30 °C for further studies.

The univariate optimization was a start point to understand the relationship between the sensitivity and the affecting parameters. The obtained results were employed to define the ranges of the chemical concentrations that must be considered by CCD optimization method.

The CCD method as the effective experimental design technique and a strategy to gather more empirical knowledge from the analysis of few experimental data was used for process analysis, modeling and optimization of the chemical variables [60] after identification of the significant chemical variables in the univariate optimization method.

Table 2 shows three considered factors, their coded and actual values in the CCD pattern. A fixed concentration of hypochlorite (13.7 mg L−1) and the univariate optimization FIA parameters were applied through all the experiments. The numbers of required experiments in the CCD optimization procedure with two replicates for the three affecting chemicals were 40. Table S1 shows the values of the coded parameters and their corresponding measured absorbance at 423 nm. Table 3 and S2 present the results of the analysis of variance (ANOVA) obtained by the CCD optimization procedure. These statistics show the relationship between the two factors and the response at the center level of the other factors. Acquired results have been shown in Fig. 5 . The best fitted model for the measured absorbance is given as:
(1)
A = C o n s t a n t +
β 1
X 1
+
β 2
X 2
+
β 3
X 3
-
β 11
X 1 2
-
β 33
X 3 2

The positive values in Eq. (1) show that the absorbance increases by the factors levels. The ANOVA results in Table 3 and S2 demonstrate that the model is significant whereas the lack of fit is not significant. The results indeed support the validity of the model. According to the equation, all of the factors (X1, X2 and X3) affect the response whereas the presence of their interactions (X1X2, X1X3 and X2X3) is rejected.

According to the equation, the coefficient of X1, X2 and X3 are positive implying that the sensitivity increases when concentrations of DNPH, hydrochloric acid and GQDs are increased. Beside the results, X1 and X3 behave differently from X2. DNPH (X1) and GQDs (X3) concentrations have a strong positive linear effect on the response. Also, they have significant negative quadratic effects on the response. It means that the sensitivity increases with the increase of these parameters, and then it reaches a maximum. Thereafter, higher concentrations of DNPH and GQDs cause lower sensitivities. Three-dimensional (3D) response surfaces in Fig. 5 clearly explain that the concentration of DNPH in the solution up to 0.9 mmol L−1 improves the sensitivity. This behavior is attributed to the more efficient formation of diazonium cation at this DNPH concentration. Likewise, GQDs, as a coupling agent, up to 2.53 mg L−1 (2.98 fold diluted) causes more sensitivity. We should note that higher GQDs concentrations tend to decrease the sensitivity.

However, by applying CCD method the proposed optimum values of DNPH, hydrochloric acid and GQDs concentrations were 0.9 mmol L−1, 0.50 mol L−1 and 2.53 mg mL−1 (2.98 fold diluted of the GQDs stock solution), respectively.

The results obtained by the univariate and the CCD optimization methods were compared with each other. For injection of a 13.7 mg L−1 hypochlorite, the optimum chemical concentrations proposed by CCD method and by the univariate method were separately applied on the assembled FIA system. The measured absorbances were 1.028 ± 0.002 and 0.879 ± 0.005 (± amounts are standard deviations) for CCD method and univariate method, respectively. The paired t-test confirmed the presence of a meaningful difference. Therefore, the proposed chemical concentrations by the CCD method were selected for the next studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of different chemicals as coupling agents
ce:section.ce:para: Sensitivity of a reagent for colorimetric assay of an analyte depends on the molar extinction of the product and the rate of the product formation. In the FIA system used for hypochlorite assay, the oxidation of DNPH by hypochlorite is very fast. Therefore, sensitivity is affected mainly by the efficiency of the coupling reaction. Under optimized condition of the research, different reagents were examined as the coupling agents (NED and 1-naphthol were used because they are two usual coupling agents). The results in Table 4 clearly reveal some facts about this GQDs based colorimetric work. First, GQDs react faster than NED with the diazotized DNPH (the molar extinction of the NED based azo dye is about 58,000 Lit cm−1 mol-1 [61]). Second, GODs do not react with the diazotized DNPH to prepare the colored product (may be due to the complete oxidation of carbons along the edges of the GODs). Third, in the acidic stream of the FIA set up, 1-naphthol is not coupled with the diazotized DNPH but GQDs are coupled (GQDs and 1-naphthol have hydroxyl functional groups on their aromatic rings and may be classified as phenolic coupling agents). Finally, citric acid as a residue (in the preparation of GQDs) has no effect on the color development.

Analytical dynamic range for the hypochlorite determination method was obtained under optimum condition. The calibration plot is given in Fig. S3 and it is observed that the absorbance was linear in the hypochlorite concentration range of 0.2–12.3 mg L−1.

Also, intra-day and inter-day reliability of the method were evaluated. The obtained results in Table 5 show a satisfactory repeatability and recovery for hypochlorite determination by the presented method.

The effects of various ions on the determination of hypochlorite as 1.0 μg mL−1 were investigated when other ions were added individually. The tolerance limit for each added ion was considered as the ion concentration that causes an error in the range of ±7% [62]. The obtained results showed Fe(III), Al(III), Cd(II) and Br− did not interfere at 400-fold (wt/wt). The other tested ions and their interferences were as: ClO4 − and Pb(II) (400-fold), CH3COO− (200-fold), Cu(II), Mg(II) and NO3 − (50-fold). The results show the reasonable selectivity of the method.

One tap water sample was analyzed by the FI method. The tap water sample showed no amount of hypochlorite. Thereafter, the tap water sample spiked with two different concentrations of hypochlorite (1.0 μg mL−1 and 2.0 μg mL−1), and then was analyzed (n = 7). The obtained recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 98.7 and 97.9%, and 4.5 and 2.8%, respectively.

Two swimming pool water samples and five bleaching solutions were also analyzed by the established procedure and two reference spectrophotometric and iodometric [42] methods. The results of the study are given in Tables 6 and 7
. The paired statistical t-test [63] was applied to investigate the reliability of the GQDs based FI method. As it can be seen, for all of the samples, tcalculated is less than tcritical. This means that there are no statistical differences between the results obtained by the established method and the two reference methods.

The hypochlorite assay method has been compared with various spectroscopic hypochlorite nanoparticle-based determination methods in Table 8 . The methods are preferred against the presented method only with respect to sensitivity. However, higher sensitivity is not the most favorable parameter for analysis of the samples containing high concentrations of an analyte. The presented GQDs based method shows many advantages on cost, speed of analysis, selectivity and range of the sample types. The presented method can be easily assembled in a general laboratory for determination of hypochlorite in various aquatic environmental and industrial samples.

GQDs, as a laboratory made reagent, has been introduced as a new colorimetric reagent for hypochlorite determination. This study has been conducted by assembling a simple three channel flow injection set up. GQDs showed fast coupling reaction with the formed diazonium cation after the instantaneous oxidation of 2, 4-dinitrophenylhydrazine by hypochlorite in a hydrochloric acid stream.

The results of the central composition design optimization showed that concentration of all of the chemical reagents were significant but their interactions were not. Increasing of hydrochloric acid increased the sensitivity continuously, but the squares of DNPH and GQDs concentrations affect the absorbance with negative β coefficients.

DFT calculations were carried out on a model structure of grapheme quantum dot (GQD). Then, the DFT results were used to calculate the electron density on the carbons (along the edges of the GQD molecule) to identify the active positions of the GQD against the electrophilic attacks. The results led to a better understanding of the route of the reaction between the diazotized DNPH and GQDs. The results showed that the best positions for attacking an electrophile (like a diazonium cation of DNPH) to GQD are the ortho positions with respect to the phenolic OH groups.

As far as we know, some of the most efficient coupling reagents are mainly aryl amines and phenolics. Some of the reagents are classified as carcinogens, potentially carcinogens or unsafe for the environment. GQDs, as defined by the presented method, establishes an efficient hypochlorite determination method with high sample throughput (100 h−1) and environmental safety.
The materials used, sensing technique (flow injection-spectrophotometry) and mechanism of the color formation make the basis of the analytical method to be applied in the future related works.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Somayyeh Baghi Sefidan was born in 1986 in Tabriz (Iran). She received the M.Sc. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2012 from Tabriz University. Her research interests include analytical aspects of carbon nanomaterials and adsorbents.

Habibollah Eskandari is an associate professor in the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili. He obtained his Ph. D. from Isfahan University of Technology in 2002. He mainly engaged in the preparation and application of nanomaterials and magnetic materials in analytical chemistry.

Amir Nasser Shamkhali is an associate professor in the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili. He received his B.Sc. degree of Applied Chemistry from Department of Chemistry, University of Tabriz in 2002. Then, he earned M.Sc. and Ph.D. degrees of Physical Chemistry from Sharif University of Technology in 2004, and 2010, respectively. His research interests are Computational Chemistry and theoretical studies on the molecules, surfaces, interfaces, nanomaterials, and excited states of molecular systems.
